text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum at the retrocecal recess, exposing underlying structures for further dissection.
The surgeon performs an inferior to superior mobilization of the cecum and ascending colon, adhering to the complete mesocolic excision (CME) principle.
The surgeon utilizes monopolar cautery and ultrasonic shear as energy devices during the laparoscopic procedure, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes an avascular surgical plane along Toll's line, revealing the duodenum, pancreas, and right kidney.
The surgeon performs a lateral to medial dissection, progressively exposing the posterior side of the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon continues dissection up to the subhepatic area, performing hepatic mobilization to further expose the anatomical structures in this region.
The surgeon incises the visceral peritoneum anterior to the central vessels, performing primary vascular control and DIII lymphadenectomy along the superior mesenteric vessels.
The surgeon utilizes a multifunctional ultrasonic device to dissect, grasp, cauterize, seal, and cut vessels, demonstrating its versatility in handling various surgical tasks.
The surgeon performs extensive lymph node dissection around the superior mesenteric vessels, utilizing the previously established lateral to medial approach.
The surgeon divides and ligates the ileocolic artery at its root using a hemolog clip, ensuring vascular control in the surgical field.
The surgeon ligates and transects the ileocolic vein at its root, where it drains into the superior mesenteric vein.
Following lymph node dissection around the middle colic vessels, the surgeon identifies the branches of the middle colic artery, visually distinguishing these vascular structures.
The surgeon controls bleeding from the right branch of the middle colic artery using a multifunctional ultrasonic device, achieving secure vessel sealing without the use of surgical clips.

The final intraoperative view shows the complete mesocolic excision and central vascular ligation achieved through reduced port laparoscopic access for right-sided colon cancer. The surgical field is clear, with visible excised mesocolic tissue and ligated central vessels.
The surgeon performs a side-to-side intracorporeal anastomosis using an articulated laparoscopic stapler, joining two sections of the bowel within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon closes the common entry hole using continuous knotless barbed sutures, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
